adventure.explanation: The 1000 km route from Santander to Riba-Roja de Túria crosses diverse landscapes including Cantabria's mountains, Iberian plateaus' deserts, and Mediterranean coasts, passing through picturesque villages like Liérganes, Albarracín, and Vinuesa.[1][2]

altitude.explanation: Cyclists reach over 1800m at the route's highest point and pass through Valdelinares, Spain's highest village at 1692m, in the Sierra de Espadán.[2]

climate.explanation: Starting on the Cantabrian coast in Santander, the route transitions from cool northern mountains to warmer Mediterranean climates near Valencia.[1][2]

community.explanation: As the very first edition opening registrations on 16 October 2025, the event has limited time to build a large rider community for the 2026 race.[1]

elevation.explanation: The full 1000 km course features +14000 m of climbing, including iconic passes like Portillo de la Lunada and Puerto del Oronet.[1][2]

expenses.explanation: Entry for the 1000 km solo category costs €359 early bird or €309 standard, with duo and team options reducing per-person fees to €279.[1]

experience.explanation: Semi-autonomous format with dedicated support team ensures comfort over the 1000 km from Santander through breathtaking Spanish landscapes to Riba-Roja.[1]

field_depth.explanation: Offering solo, duo, and team-of-4 categories across 200 km to 1000 km distances dilutes depth in any single competitive class for the inaugural 2026 event.[1]

length.explanation: The flagship route spans 1000 km (~621 miles) from Santander on the northern coast to Riba-Roja de Túria near Valencia.[1][2]

logistics.explanation: Departure from Santander and arrival in Riba-Roja de Túria, both near airports, simplify travel for the 2026 edition.[1]

race_quality.explanation: The route showcases emblematic climbs like Puerto de Almedijar and Puerto del Oronet in Valencia's Sierra Calderona, blending endurance with stunning vistas.[2]

technicality.explanation: Riders tackle demanding passes such as Portillo de la Lunada in Cantabria and Puerto del Oronet in Sierra Calderona, mixing climbs with varied terrain.[2]

value.explanation: At €309 for 1000 km solo (~621 miles with 14,000m elevation), it delivers a coast-to-coast Spanish adventure at competitive pricing versus distance.[1]

cultural_impact.explanation: Showcases Spain's pueblos bonitos, parks, and Vuelta-used climbs, boosting local cycling culture.

prestige.explanation: As the first edition of Race Across Spain in 2026, it lacks the established history of multi-year ultra-cycling events.[1][3]

course_description.character: A grand traverse of Spain from the wild Cantabrian north to Valencia's Mediterranean warmth, blending relentless climbs, picturesque villages, and diverse terrains through mountains, plateaus, and coasts.

suffering_zones.label: Portillo de la Lunada
suffering_zones.description: Iconic 30km Cantabrian pass with steep gradients and exposed high-altitude scenery, a Vuelta a España regular.

suffering_zones.label: Iberian Plateau
suffering_zones.description: Arid, rolling expanses demanding sustained power and heat management midway.

suffering_zones.label: Puerto de Almedijar & Oronet
suffering_zones.description: Final Valencian Sierra climbs testing fatigued legs with technical descents to the coast.

course_description.signature_challenge: Overcoming the cumulative 35,000ft elevation across 621 miles, punctuated by Spain's most scenic passes and cultural gems.

logistics.overview: Semi-autonomous ultra with dedicated support: PGO tracking, living bases for rest/refuel, bib collection, briefings, and awards at Palacio de los Deportes in Santander.
logistics.accommodation: Sleeping areas at living bases; Santander offers hotels near start/finish venue.
logistics.getting_there: Santander (SDR) and Valencia (VLC) airports nearby start/end; Riba-Roja de Túria close to Valencia.
logistics.parking: Available at Palacio de los Deportes main venue in Santander.
logistics.spectator_info: Family/friends welcome at start/finish in Santander and key living bases; awards at 10am.

climate.overview: Mild late-summer conditions ideal for ultra, starting cool/green in Cantabria, warming to dry Mediterranean south.
climate.wind_conditions: Moderate coastal/ocean winds north, lighter inland; potential headwinds on plateaus.
climate.altitude_effects: Noticeable above 1500-1800m on high passes; cooler temps and thinner air.

terrain.terrain_overview: Primarily paved roads winding through Cantabrian mountains, Iberian plateaus, and Valencian sierras; suitable for road or gravel bikes.
terrain.key_features: Portillo de la Lunada pass, Pueblos más Bonitos like Liérganes/Vinuesa/Albarracín, Cabárceno Park, highest village Valdelinares (1692m), Sierra Espadán/Calderona climbs.

history.overview: New flagship event in Race Across Series, launching Spain's ultra-cycling scene with inaugural 2025/2026 editions drawing international talent.
history.notable_editions: 2025 first edition featured Pauline Gaidet win in 300km scratch; 2024 podcast highlights early Santander successes.
history.growth_trajectory: Rapid expansion with 200-1000km options, strict qualifiers for long distances signaling rising prestige.

final_verdict.one_liner: Spain's ultimate road ultra odyssey: 621 miles of mountains, culture, and unbreakable spirit.
final_verdict.best_for: Endurance purists craving epic trans-peninsular adventure with top support.
final_verdict.not_ideal_for: Gravel purists (mostly paved) or prestige hunters (still emerging).
final_verdict.pro_tip: Qualify via prior Race Across events; pack for 1800m+ chills and southern heat—versatile kit wins.
